summary: Stancil argues that while the back of the modern data stack has converged on clear architectural norms, the consumption layer (analytics/BI) is fragmented and unsettled. This diversity reflects real differences in people and business use cases, yet the industry still lacks shared standards and is muddied by vendor incentives. He expects eventual consolidation, driven by community consensus or economic gravity, even if innovation thrives in today’s chaos.
money_quotes: But the front quarter of the ship—analytics and BI tools for data consumption—the front is a very different story.
There’s barely a shared understanding of what companies should be doing with the data they have, much less how they should do it.
Helping people use data effectively is the hard problem.
This makes the conversation about the consumption layer a campaign, and proxies opinions about architectures through specific products.
Technology trends are often cyclical, swinging back and forth from centralized and decentralized, from bundled and unbundled.
key_insight: The analytics consumption layer is fragmented because human and business needs vary, but it will likely consolidate toward shared standards as economic and community forces reassert order.
